museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: March 2008: Super Smash Bros. Brawl launches in North America while Europe waits.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for March 2008. Regional timing and UK retail context should be checked before verification.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

items.title: Super Smash Bros. Brawl launches in North America while Europe waits.
items.body: A period-context note for the month, written cautiously where the evidence is broader than one exact day.

items.body: A period-context note for the month, written cautiously where the evidence is broader than one exact day.

items.title: The idea of Nintendo's back catalogue as a playable archive becomes obvious.
items.body: A period-context note for the month, written cautiously where the evidence is broader than one exact day.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates should be rechecked during human edit.

items.title: Digital stores, WiiWare, XBLA, PSN and the App Store make small downloadable games more visible.
items.body: A year-level context marker included to frame the month without claiming a new event happened on a specific day.

items.title: Wii Fit and Mario Kart keep Nintendo broad while HD consoles chase prestige blockbusters.
items.body: A year-level context marker included to frame the month without claiming a new event happened on a specific day.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Super Smash Bros. Brawl
items.body: Nintendo's mascot archive becomes a disc full of arguments, unlocks and music.
items.sourceNote: Regional timing varies; Europe follows in June 2008.

items.title: Mario Kart Wii
items.body: Plastic wheels and online races turn the Wii into a louder family object.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for April 2008.

items.title: Grand Theft Auto IV
items.body: Liberty City becomes denser, darker and impossible to ignore.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for April 29, 2008.

items.title: Wii Fit
items.body: Nintendo turns a bathroom-scale peripheral into a mainstream living-room ritual.
items.sourceNote: Regional timing varies; Europe launches in April 2008.

items.title: Metal Gear Solid 4: Guns of the Patriots
items.body: Kojima gives PS3 a technical showcase and a long goodbye.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for June 12, 2008.

items.title: Braid
items.body: A downloadable puzzle game becomes a symbol of Xbox Live Arcade's artistic potential.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for August 2008 on Xbox Live Arcade.

items.title: LittleBigPlanet
items.body: Sony turns user creation into sackcloth charm and platform identity.
items.sourceNote: Regional timing varies in late October/early November 2008.

items.title: Fallout 3
items.body: Bethesda turns the wasteland into a first-person pilgrimage.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for October 2008.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: WiiWare launches in Europe
items.body: Nintendo adds a download shelf to Wii, making small games part of the console's public face.
items.sourceNote: Nintendo Life reports May 20, 2008.

items.title: Apple App Store opens
items.body: The iPhone App Store changes the future of mobile games, even if the full effect is not immediate.
items.sourceNote: Apple's July 2008 release confirms App Store availability.

items.title: PSN and XBLA mature
items.body: Downloadable games such as Braid and PSN/XBLA releases make digital storefronts feel culturally important.
items.sourceNote: Year-level digital store context; verify individual storefront dates as needed.

items.title: Wii Balance Board becomes mainstream hardware
items.body: Wii Fit turns a peripheral into a household object discussed beyond games media.
items.sourceNote: Wii Fit 2008 regional launch context supports this reading.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Services and servers
items.body: Braid, World of Goo, WiiWare, XBLA and PSN make downloadable games feel like real releases rather than extras.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Friends and parties
items.body: GTA IV's online modes, Left 4 Dead and Gears 2 show co-op and multiplayer becoming routine expectations.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Downloads and stores
items.body: The App Store opens a new mobile game frontier that few traditional players fully understand yet.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Everyday connection
items.body: Achievements, trophies, profiles and patches become part of how games are remembered.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like the disc was still king, but the menu was becoming a museum corridor of its own.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: GTA IV and Fallout 3 offered huge worlds, while Braid and World of Goo suggested smaller downloads could matter just as much.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: Wii Fit made games visible in newspapers, living rooms and conversations that did not sound like gaming conversations.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: The App Store was easy to underestimate in the moment; it looked like phone software, not a future earthquake.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
